title: Anfield

infobox.value: is a [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") stadium in the area of [Anfield](/wiki/Anfield_(suburb) "Anfield (suburb)"), Liverpool, England, which has been the home of [Liverpool F.C.](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C.") since its formation in 1892. The stadium has a [seating capacity](/wiki/Seating_capacity "Seating capacity") of 61,276, making it the [fifth-largest](/wiki/List_of_football_stadiums_in_England "List of football stadiums in England") football stadium in England.[[2]](#cite_note-2024_capacity-2) It was originally the home of [Everton](/wiki/Everton_F.C. "Everton F.C.") from 1884 to 1891 before they moved to [Goodison Park](/wiki/Goodison_Park "Goodison Park") after a dispute with the club president.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

**Anfield** is a [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") stadium in the area of [Anfield](/wiki/Anfield_(suburb) "Anfield (suburb)"), Liverpool, England, which has been the home of [Liverpool F.C.](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C.") since its formation in 1892. The stadium has a [seating capacity](/wiki/Seating_capacity "Seating capacity") of 61,276, making it the [fifth-largest](/wiki/List_of_football_stadiums_in_England "List of football stadiums in England") football stadium in England.[[2]](#cite_note-2024_capacity-2) It was originally the home of [Everton](/wiki/Everton_F.C. "Everton F.C.") from 1884 to 1891 before they moved to [Goodison Park](/wiki/Goodison_Park "Goodison Park") after a dispute with the club president.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The stadium has four stands: the [Spion Kop](/wiki/Spion_Kop_(stadiums) "Spion Kop (stadiums)"), the Main Stand, the Sir Kenny Dalglish Stand, and the Anfield Road End.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The record attendance of 61,905 was set at a match between Liverpool and [Wolverhampton Wanderers](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Wanderers_F.C. "Wolverhampton Wanderers F.C.") in 1952.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The ground converted to an [all-seater stadium](/wiki/All-seater_stadium "All-seater stadium") in 1994 as a result of the [Taylor Report](/wiki/Taylor_Report "Taylor Report"), which reduced its capacity.

Two gates at the stadium are named after former Liverpool managers: [Bill Shankly](/wiki/Bill_Shankly "Bill Shankly") and [Bob Paisley](/wiki/Bob_Paisley "Bob Paisley"). Both managers have been honoured with statues outside the stadium: Shankly's unveiled in 1997 by the Kop Stand and Paisley's in 2020 by the Main Stand. The ground is 2 miles (3 km) from [Liverpool Lime Street railway station](/wiki/Liverpool_Lime_Street_railway_station "Liverpool Lime Street railway station").

It was proposed in 2002 for the club to relocate to a new stadium in the adjacent [Stanley Park](/wiki/Stanley_Park_Stadium "Stanley Park Stadium"), but after the acquisition of Liverpool by [Fenway Sports Group](/wiki/Fenway_Sports_Group "Fenway Sports Group") in 2010, it was made clear this would not happen, with the decision being taken to expand Anfield instead. Construction for an extension to the main stand began on 8 December 2014.[[7]](#cite_note-workstarts-7) This extension, one of the largest all-seater single stands in European football, opened to the public on 9 September 2016, increasing the stadium capacity to 54,074.[[8]](#cite_note-cap2022-8) The Anfield Road Stand redevelopment completed in 2024, increased the stadium capacity to over 61,000.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anfield&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Houlding_memorial%2C_Liverpool_FC_1.jpg/250px-Houlding_memorial%2C_Liverpool_FC_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Houlding_memorial,_Liverpool_FC_1.jpg)

Memorial to [John Houlding](/wiki/John_Houlding "John Houlding") (1833–1902) outside Anfield on the 125th anniversary of [Liverpool F.C.](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C.")

The stadium is named after the surrounding area, [Anfield](/wiki/Anfield_(suburb) "Anfield (suburb)"). The word originated in the combination of [Old](/wiki/Old_English "Old English") and [Middle English](/wiki/Middle_English "Middle English") words, which mean "a field on a slope". Anfield, and its deviations, has been associated with the area since at least 1642.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) It has been suggested that the name is linked to the [influx of Irish people](/wiki/Irish_diaspora "Irish diaspora") into the spreading city in the 1850s, and was associated with Annefield, outside [New Ross](/wiki/New_Ross "New Ross"), County Wexford, Ireland.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Opened in 1884, Anfield was originally owned by John Orrell, a minor landowner who was a friend of [Everton](/wiki/Everton_F.C. "Everton F.C.") member [John Houlding](/wiki/John_Houlding "John Houlding").[[13]](#cite_note-13) Everton, who previously played at Priory Road, were in need of a new venue owing to the noise produced by the crowd on match days.[[14]](#cite_note-LFC-14) Orrell lent the pitch to the club in exchange for a small rent. The first match at the ground was between Everton and Earlestown on 28 September 1884, which Everton won 5–0.[[15]](#cite_note-15) During Everton's tenure at the stadium, stands were erected for some of the 8,000-plus spectators regularly attending matches, although the ground was capable of holding around 20,000 spectators and occasionally did. The ground was considered of international standard at the time, playing host to the [British Home Championship](/wiki/British_Home_Championship "British Home Championship") match between [England](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team") and [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland_national_football_team_(1882%E2%80%931950) "Ireland national football team (1882–1950)") in 1889. Anfield's first league match was played on 8 September 1888, between Everton and [Accrington F.C.](/wiki/Accrington_F.C. "Accrington F.C.") Everton quickly improved as a team, and became Anfield's first league champions in the [1890–91](/wiki/1890%E2%80%9391_in_English_football "1890–91 in English football") season.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

In 1892, negotiations to purchase the land at Anfield from Orrell escalated into a dispute between Houlding and the Everton committee over how the club was run. Events culminated in Everton's move to [Goodison Park](/wiki/Goodison_Park "Goodison Park").[[14]](#cite_note-LFC-14) Houlding was left with an empty stadium, and decided to form a new club to occupy it. The new team was called [Liverpool F.C. and Athletic Grounds Ltd](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C."), and the club's first match at Anfield was a [friendly](/wiki/Exhibition_game "Exhibition game") played in front of 200 people on 1 September 1892, against [Rotherham Town](/wiki/Rotherham_Town_F.C._(1878) "Rotherham Town F.C. (1878)"). Liverpool won 7–1.[[17]](#cite_note-YNWA-17)

[![A white flagpole in front of football ground](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/FlagpoleGreatEasternLFC.jpg/250px-FlagpoleGreatEasternLFC.jpg)](/wiki/File:FlagpoleGreatEasternLFC.jpg)

The [topmast](/wiki/Topmast "Topmast") rescued from the [SS *Great Eastern*](/wiki/SS_Great_Eastern "SS Great Eastern")

Liverpool's first [Football League](/wiki/English_Football_League "English Football League") match at Anfield was played on 9 September 1893, against [Lincoln City](/wiki/Lincoln_City_F.C. "Lincoln City F.C."). Liverpool won 4–0 in front of 5,000 spectators.[[18]](#cite_note-18) A new stand capable of holding 3,000 spectators was constructed in 1895 on the site of the present Main Stand. Designed by architect [Archibald Leitch](/wiki/Archibald_Leitch "Archibald Leitch"),[[19]](#cite_note-19) the stand had a distinctive red and white [gable](/wiki/Gable "Gable"), and was similar to the main stand at [Newcastle United](/wiki/Newcastle_United_F.C. "Newcastle United F.C.")'s ground [St James' Park](/wiki/St_James%27_Park "St James' Park").[[17]](#cite_note-YNWA-17) Another stand was constructed at the Anfield Road end in 1903, built from timber and [corrugated iron](/wiki/Corrugated_iron "Corrugated iron"). After Liverpool had won their second League championship in [1906](/wiki/1905%E2%80%9306_in_English_football "1905–06 in English football"), a new stand was built along the Walton Breck Road. Local journalist Ernest Edwards, who was the sports editor of newspapers the *[Liverpool Daily Post](/wiki/Liverpool_Daily_Post "Liverpool Daily Post")* and *[Echo](/wiki/Liverpool_Echo "Liverpool Echo")*, named it the [Spion Kop](/wiki/Spion_Kop_(stadiums) "Spion Kop (stadiums)"); it was named after a [famous hill](/wiki/Battle_of_Spion_Kop "Battle of Spion Kop") in South Africa where a local regiment had suffered heavy losses during the [Boer War](/wiki/Boer_War "Boer War") in 1900. More than 300 men had died, many of them from Liverpool, as the British army attempted to capture the strategic hilltop. Around the same period a stand was also built along Kemlyn Road.[[20]](#cite_note-Kelly_117-20)

On 14 February 1921, Scouse comedian [Harry Weldon](/wiki/Harry_Weldon_(comedian) "Harry Weldon (comedian)") invited [Dick, Kerr Ladies](/wiki/Dick,_Kerr_Ladies "Dick, Kerr Ladies") to Anfield to compete in a match between themselves and a side made up of the best players from the rest of Britain, for The Harry Weldon Cup in aid of unemployed ex-servicemen. Weldon's side was made up of the best individual players from teams throughout the UK, with one player even travelling from the island of Unst in the Shetland Isles. 25,000 fans were in attendance that day as Dick, Kerr Ladies went on to emphatically win 9–1. The trophy is now on display at the [National Football Museum](/wiki/National_Football_Museum "National Football Museum") in Manchester.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25)

The ground remained much the same until 1928, when the Kop was redesigned and extended to hold 30,000 spectators, all standing. A roof was erected as well.[[26]](#cite_note-26) Many stadia in England had stands named after the Spion Kop. Anfield's was the largest Kop in the country at the time—it was able to hold more supporters than some entire football grounds.[[27]](#cite_note-Echo-27) In the same year the [topmast](/wiki/Topmast "Topmast") of the [SS *Great Eastern*](/wiki/SS_Great_Eastern "SS Great Eastern"), one of the first iron ships, was rescued from the [ship breaking](/wiki/Ship_breaking "Ship breaking") yard at nearby [Rock Ferry](/wiki/Rock_Ferry "Rock Ferry"), and was hauled up Everton Valley by a team of horses, to be erected alongside the new Kop. It still stands there, serving as a flag pole.[[20]](#cite_note-Kelly_117-20)

[![Photograph of a stand full of people. In front of the stand is a field and a goal.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/The_Kop_-_geograph.org.uk_-_702695.jpg/250px-The_Kop_-_geograph.org.uk_-_702695.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Kop_-_geograph.org.uk_-_702695.jpg)

The Kop in 1974, before the [Taylor Report](/wiki/Taylor_Report "Taylor Report") recommended standing areas in football grounds be outlawed following the [Hillsborough disaster](/wiki/Hillsborough_disaster "Hillsborough disaster") in 1989.

[Floodlights](/wiki/Floodlight "Floodlight") were installed at a cost of £12,000 in 1957. On 30 October they were switched on for the first time for a match against Everton to commemorate the 75-year anniversary of the [Liverpool County Football Association](/wiki/Liverpool_County_Football_Association "Liverpool County Football Association").[[27]](#cite_note-Echo-27) In 1963 the old Kemlyn Road stand was replaced by a [cantilevered](/wiki/Cantilever "Cantilever") stand, built at a cost of £350,000, accommodating 6,700 spectators.[[28]](#cite_note-inglis-28) Two years later alterations were made at the Anfield Road end, turning it into a larger covered standing area with refreshments under the structure. The biggest redevelopment came in 1973, when the old Main Stand was partially demolished and extended backward with new roof. Simultaneously the concrete pylon floodlights were demolished with new lights installed along the rooflines of the Kemlyn Road and Main Stands. The new stand was officially opened by [the Duke of Kent](/wiki/Prince_Edward,_Duke_of_Kent "Prince Edward, Duke of Kent") on 10 March 1973.[[28]](#cite_note-inglis-28) In the 1980s the paddock in front of the Main Stand was turned into seating, and in 1982 seats were introduced at the Anfield Road end. The Shankly Gates were erected in 1982, a tribute to former manager [Bill Shankly](/wiki/Bill_Shankly "Bill Shankly"); his widow Nessie unlocked them for the first time on 26 August 1982.[[20]](#cite_note-Kelly_117-20) Across the Shankly Gates are the words *You'll Never Walk Alone*, the title of the [hit song](/wiki/You%27ll_Never_Walk_Alone "You'll Never Walk Alone") by [Gerry and the Pacemakers](/wiki/Gerry_and_the_Pacemakers "Gerry and the Pacemakers") adopted by Liverpool fans as the club's anthem during Shankly's time as manager.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

Coloured seats and a police room were added to the Kemlyn Road stand in 1987. After the [Hillsborough disaster](/wiki/Hillsborough_disaster "Hillsborough disaster") in 1989 when Police mismanagement led to overcrowding and the deaths of 97 Liverpool fans, the [Taylor Report](/wiki/Taylor_Report "Taylor Report") recommended that all grounds in the country should be converted into all-seater grounds by May 1994.[[30]](#cite_note-30) A second tier was added to the Kemlyn Road stand in 1992, turning it into a double-decker layout. It included [executive boxes](/wiki/Executive_boxes "Executive boxes") and [function suites](/wiki/Conference_hall "Conference hall") as well as 11,000 seating spaces. Plans to expand the stand had been made earlier, with the club buying up houses on Kemlyn Road during the 1970s, and 1980s, but had to be put on hold until 1990 because two sisters,[[31]](#cite_note-31) Joan and Nora Mason, refused to sell their house. When the club reached an agreement with the sisters in 1990, the expansion plans were put into action.[[32]](#cite_note-32) The stand—renamed the Centenary Stand—was officially opened on 1 September 1992 by [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA") president [Lennart Johansson](/wiki/Lennart_Johansson "Lennart Johansson"). The Kop was rebuilt in 1994 after the recommendations of the Taylor Report and became all seated; it is still a single tier, and the capacity was significantly reduced to 12,390.[[27]](#cite_note-Echo-27)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/You%27ll_Never_Walk_Alone_%2813976345652%29.jpg/250px-You%27ll_Never_Walk_Alone_%2813976345652%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:You%27ll_Never_Walk_Alone_(13976345652).jpg)

The Shankly Gates were erected in 1982 in tribute to [Bill Shankly](/wiki/Bill_Shankly "Bill Shankly") (1913–1981)

On 4 December 1997, a bronze statue of Bill Shankly was unveiled at the visitors' centre in front of the Kop. Standing at over 8 feet (2.4 m) tall, the statue depicts Shankly with a fan's scarf around his neck, in a familiar pose he adopted when receiving applause from fans. Inscribed on the statue are the words "Bill Shankly – He Made The People Happy".[[33]](#cite_note-33) The Hillsborough memorial was situated alongside the Shankly Gates before it was moved next to 97 Avenue in front of the redeveloped main stand in 2016.[[34]](#cite_note-Memorial-34) The memorial is always decorated with flowers and tributes to the 97 people who died as a result of the disaster. At the centre of the memorial is an [eternal flame](/wiki/Eternal_flame "Eternal flame"), signifying that those who died will never be forgotten.[[34]](#cite_note-Memorial-34)

In 1998 a new two-tier Anfield Road end was opened. The stand has encountered a number of problems since its redevelopment; at the beginning of the [1999–2000](/wiki/1999%E2%80%932000_in_English_football "1999–2000 in English football") season, a series of support poles and [stanchions](/wiki/Stanchion "Stanchion") had to be brought in to give extra stability to the top tier of the stand. During [Ronnie Moran](/wiki/Ronnie_Moran "Ronnie Moran")'s [testimonial match](/wiki/Testimonial_match "Testimonial match") against [Celtic](/wiki/Celtic_F.C. "Celtic F.C."), many fans complained of movement of the top tier. At the same time that the stanchions were inserted, the executive seating area was expanded by two rows in the main stand, lowering the [seating capacity](/wiki/Seating_capacity "Seating capacity") in the paddock.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

On 30 January 2020, a bronze statue of Bob Paisley was unveiled outside the Main Stand in Paisley Square. The statue was commissioned and donated by the club's main sponsor, Standard Chartered, to celebrate the ten-year anniversary of the relationship with the club.[[36]](#cite_note-36) The statue is 8 ft (2.4 m) tall and depicts an iconic image of the club's history, Paisley carrying future club captain Emlyn Hughes off the field during a match against Tottenham Hotspur at Anfield in April 1968.[[37]](#cite_note-Statue-37)

## Structures and facilities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anfield&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Structures and facilities")]

[![diagram of a stadium](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Anfield_outline.svg/250px-Anfield_outline.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Anfield_outline.svg)

Outline of Anfield pre-2015 redevelopment; The Sir Kenny Dalglish Stand (top), The Kop (right), Main Stand (bottom) and Anfield Road stand (left)

Anfield has 60,725 seats split between four stands: the Anfield Road end, the Sir Kenny Dalglish Stand, the Kop, and the Main Stand. The Anfield Road end and Sir Kenny Dalglish Stand are two-tiered, while the Kop is single-tiered and the Main Stand three-tiered.[[38]](#cite_note-plan-38) Entry to the stadium is gained by [radio-frequency identification](/wiki/Radio-frequency_identification "Radio-frequency identification") (RFID) [smart cards](/wiki/Smart_card "Smart card") rather than the traditional staffed [turnstile](/wiki/Turnstile "Turnstile"). This system, used in all 80 turnstiles around Anfield, was introduced in 2005.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

Plans to replace Anfield with a new 60,000-capacity stadium in adjacent [Stanley Park](/wiki/Stanley_Park_Stadium "Stanley Park Stadium") were initiated in 2002.[[40]](#cite_note-40) The plans were revisited under the ownership of [Tom Hicks](/wiki/Tom_Hicks "Tom Hicks") and [George Gillett](/wiki/George_N._Gillett,_Jr. "George N. Gillett, Jr.").[[41]](#cite_note-41) Following the acquisition of Liverpool by [Fenway Sports Group](/wiki/Fenway_Sports_Group "Fenway Sports Group") (then known as New England Sports Ventures (NESV)) in 2010, the owners abandoned the proposed new stadium in Stanley Park, preferring instead to redevelop and expand Anfield,[[42]](#cite_note-42) thus echoing their decision to renovate [Fenway Park](/wiki/Fenway_Park "Fenway Park").
The [Kop](/wiki/Spion_Kop_(stadiums) "Spion Kop (stadiums)") is a large single-tiered stand. Originally a large terraced banking providing accommodation for more than 30,000 spectators, the current incarnation was constructed in 1994–95 and is single-tiered with no executive boxes. The Kop houses the club's museum, the Reducate centre and the official club shop.[[43]](#cite_note-43) The Kop is the most-renowned stand at Anfield among home and away supporters, with the people who occupy the stand referred to as *kopites*. Such is the reputation of the stand that it was claimed that the crowd in the Kop could suck the ball into the goal.[[44]](#cite_note-44) Traditionally, Liverpool's most vocal supporters congregate in this stand.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

[![steps leading down to tunnel, above the tunnel is a sign with This Is Anfield in white letters on a red background, with a crest on it](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/This_is_Anfield.jpg/250px-This_is_Anfield.jpg)](/wiki/File:This_is_Anfield.jpg)

The "THIS IS ANFIELD" sign above the old tunnel to the pitch at Anfield. Installed by former manager [Bill Shankly](/wiki/Bill_Shankly "Bill Shankly") in 1972 to instill fear into the opposition, it is now located above the new tunnel entrance.

The oldest stand at Anfield is the Main Stand, taking 76 years to complete. The stand was completed in 2016, however, the lower section dates from 1906. The bottom tier of the stand houses the directors' box. The directors' VIP box is located at the rear of the lower tier of the stand. The old large roof was supported by two thin central uprights, with a large suspended television camera gantry which has moved to the front of the third tier.[[46]](#cite_note-46) The players' tunnel and the technical area where the managers and substitutes sit during the match are in the middle of the stand at pitch level. Above the stairs leading down to the pitch hung a sign stating "THIS IS ANFIELD". Its purpose was to both intimidate the opposition and to bring the Liverpool players who touch it good luck. Liverpool players and coaching staff traditionally reached up and placed one or both hands on it as they passed underneath.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

The sign was temporarily removed during the most recent reconstruction of the Main Stand; it was placed at the exit from the new Main Stand tunnel to the pitch in advance of Liverpool's [2016–17](/wiki/2016%E2%80%9317_Liverpool_F.C._season "2016–17 Liverpool F.C. season") home opener.[[48]](#cite_note-48) Then-Liverpool manager [Jürgen Klopp](/wiki/J%C3%BCrgen_Klopp "Jürgen Klopp") had banned players from touching the restored sign until the team had won at least one major trophy.[[49]](#cite_note-49) After winning the [2019 UEFA Champions League Final](/wiki/2019_UEFA_Champions_League_Final "2019 UEFA Champions League Final") his players were allowed to do so again.[[50]](#cite_note-50) Praising the impact the Anfield atmosphere has on the home team and the effect it has on the away team, opposition manager [Pep Guardiola](/wiki/Pep_Guardiola "Pep Guardiola") stated: "The motto 'This is Anfield' is no marketing spin. There's something about it that you will find in no other stadium in the world."[[51]](#cite_note-51) Following the [2005 Champions League semi-final second leg](/wiki/2004%E2%80%9305_UEFA_Champions_League_knockout_stage#Liverpool_v_Chelsea "2004–05 UEFA Champions League knockout stage") at Anfield which Liverpool won 1–0, defeated Chelsea manager [José Mourinho](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Mourinho "José Mourinho") recognised the role Liverpool fans played on the match: "I felt the power of Anfield, it was magnificent."[[52]](#cite_note-52)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Anfield_Stadium_-_IMG_2164.JPG/250px-Anfield_Stadium_-_IMG_2164.JPG)](/wiki/File:Anfield_Stadium_-_IMG_2164.JPG)

The Paisley Gateway was erected outside the Kop in 1999 in tribute to former manager [Bob Paisley](/wiki/Bob_Paisley "Bob Paisley") (1918–1996). It includes a depiction of the three [European Cups](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") he won during his tenure.

The Sir Kenny Dalglish Stand is a two-tiered stand. Originally a single-tiered stand called the Kemlyn Road Stand, the second tier was added in 1992 to coincide with the club's centenary.[[53]](#cite_note-timeline-53) It is located opposite the Main Stand and houses directors' boxes, which are between the two tiers. The stand also houses the ground's police station.[[38]](#cite_note-plan-38) On 3 May 2017, Liverpool announced the Centenary Stand would be renamed the Kenny Dalglish Stand in honour of the club's greatest servant, former player and manager [Kenny Dalglish](/wiki/Kenny_Dalglish "Kenny Dalglish").[[54]](#cite_note-54)

The Anfield Road stand, on the left side of the Main Stand, houses the [away fans](/wiki/Home_(sports) "Home (sports)") during matches. The Anfield Road End was rebuilt in 1965, and multi-coloured seats were added in 1982. Originally a single-tier stand, a further revamp, which was completed in 1998, gave the stand a second-tier providing additional seating.[[53]](#cite_note-timeline-53)

There are 59 spaces available in the stadium to accommodate [wheelchair](/wiki/Wheelchair "Wheelchair") users who have season tickets; a further 33 spaces are available for general sale and 8 are allocated to away supporters. These spaces are located in the Main Stand, Anfield Road Stand and The Kop. There are 38 spaces available for the [visually impaired](/wiki/Visually_impaired "Visually impaired"), which are situated in the old paddock area of the Main Stand, with space for one personal assistant each. A headset with full [commentary](/wiki/Play-by-play "Play-by-play") is provided.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

The stadium features tributes to two of the club's most successful managers. The Paisley Gateway is a tribute to [Bob Paisley](/wiki/Bob_Paisley "Bob Paisley"), who guided Liverpool to three [European Cups](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") and six [League Championships](/wiki/Football_League_First_Division "Football League First Division") in the 1970s, and 1980s. The gates were erected at the Kop; their design includes representations of the three European Cups Paisley won during his tenure, the crest of his birthplace in [Hetton-le-Hole](/wiki/Hetton-le-Hole "Hetton-le-Hole"), and the crest of Liverpool.[[56]](#cite_note-56) The Shankly Gates, in tribute of Bill Shankly, Paisley's predecessor between 1959 and 1974, are at the Anfield Road end. Their design includes a Scottish flag, a Scottish thistle, the Liverpool badge, and the words "You'll Never Walk Alone".[[57]](#cite_note-57)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Anfield_panorama%2C_20_October_2012.jpg/960px-Anfield_panorama%2C_20_October_2012.jpg)](/wiki/File:Anfield_panorama,_20_October_2012.jpg "A panorama of Anfield from the Anfield Road Stand, showing from left to right the Sir Kenny Dalglish Stand, the Kop Stand and the former Main Stand in 2012")

A panorama of Anfield from the Anfield Road Stand, showing from left to right the Sir Kenny Dalglish Stand, the Kop Stand and the former Main Stand in 2012

## Redevelopment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anfield&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Redevelopment")]

### Abandoned new stadium plans

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anfield&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Abandoned new stadium plans")]

See also: [Stanley Park Stadium](/wiki/Stanley_Park_Stadium "Stanley Park Stadium")

[![A stand which is full of people standing to support their team. There are a number of flags, scarfs, and banners in the crowd.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Liverpool_v_Chelsea%2C_2005.jpg/250px-Liverpool_v_Chelsea%2C_2005.jpg)](/wiki/File:Liverpool_v_Chelsea,_2005.jpg)

*The Kop*; the atmosphere generated by the crowd in the stand led owner [John W. Henry](/wiki/John_W._Henry "John W. Henry") to reconsider the construction of a new stadium.

Plans to replace Anfield were originally started by Liverpool in May 2002.[[58]](#cite_note-BBCSport1-58) The proposed capacity was 55,000, but it was later changed to 61,000, with 1,000 seats given for segregation between home and away fans. Several attempts were made between 2003 and 2007 by the [Liverpool City Council](/wiki/Liverpool_City_Council "Liverpool City Council") to instigate a [groundshare](/wiki/Groundshare "Groundshare") of the proposed stadium with local rivals Everton, but this move was rejected, as neither club favoured it.[[59]](#cite_note-59) On 30 July 2004 Liverpool was granted planning permission to build a new stadium 300 yards (270 m) away from Anfield at Stanley Park.[[60]](#cite_note-60) On 8 September 2006, Liverpool City Council agreed to grant the club a [999-year lease](/wiki/999-year_lease "999-year lease") of the land on the proposed site.[[61]](#cite_note-61)

Following the takeover of Liverpool on 6 February 2007 by [George Gillett](/wiki/George_N._Gillett,_Jr. "George N. Gillett, Jr.") and [Tom Hicks](/wiki/Tom_Hicks "Tom Hicks"), the proposed stadium was redesigned. In November 2007, the redesigned layout was approved by the council, and construction was due to start in early 2008.[[62]](#cite_note-62) The new stadium, provisionally called [Stanley Park Stadium](/wiki/Stanley_Park_Stadium "Stanley Park Stadium"), was to be built by [HKS](/wiki/HKS,_Inc. "HKS, Inc."). It was scheduled to open in August 2011 with a capacity of 60,000.[[63]](#cite_note-63) If the new stadium had been built, Anfield would have been demolished. The land would have become home to the centrepiece for the Anfield Plaza development, which would have included a hotel, restaurants and offices.[[64]](#cite_note-64) However, the construction of Stanley Park was delayed following the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis") and the [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession"), which directly affected the then American owners. The situation was worsened because the club was bought with [borrowed money](/wiki/Bank_loan "Bank loan"), not the owners' [capital](/wiki/Financial_capital "Financial capital"), and interest rates were higher than expected.[[65]](#cite_note-HicksCreditCrunch-65) Hicks and Gillett promised to begin work on the stadium within 60 days of acquisition of the club, but had trouble financing the estimated £500 million needed for the Stanley Park development. The deadline passed and the plan was eventually cancelled by the Fenway Sports Group, as their preference was to re-develop Anfield.[[66]](#cite_note-Redevelop-66)

### Anfield redevelopment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anfield&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Anfield redevelopment")]

The acquisition of Liverpool by [Fenway Sports Group](/wiki/Fenway_Sports_Group "Fenway Sports Group") in October 2010 put into question whether Liverpool would leave Anfield. In February 2011, the new club owner, [John W. Henry](/wiki/John_W._Henry "John W. Henry"), stated he had a preference for staying at Anfield and expanding the capacity. After attending a number of games at Anfield, Henry stated that "the Kop is unrivalled", adding "it would be hard to replicate that feeling anywhere else".[[66]](#cite_note-Redevelop-66)
On 15 October 2012, Liverpool City Council announced plans to regenerate the Anfield area after securing a £25m grant, with a housing association also set to invest.[[67]](#cite_note-67)[[68]](#cite_note-68)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Main_Stand_expansion%2C_Anfield%2C_Liverpool_%28geograph_4920674%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Main_Stand_expansion%2C_Anfield%2C_Liverpool_%28geograph_4920674%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Main_Stand_expansion,_Anfield,_Liverpool_(geograph_4920674)_(cropped).jpg)

The Main Stand redevelopment in March 2016

On 23 August 2013, Anfield was listed as an Asset of Community Value by Liverpool City Council.[[69]](#cite_note-69) On 11 September, current owner John W. Henry announced that they had the funds to afford the expansion, but they are waiting for the City Council to finalise the purchase of houses in the area before they commit to plans to expand the Main Stand and the Anfield Road end of the ground.[[70]](#cite_note-70)
In April 2014, Liverpool signed a legal agreement with Liverpool City Council and Your Housing Group to redevelop the surrounding Anfield area. This was seen as a significant step towards the renovation of the stadium. The redevelopment was worth around £260 million.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

#### Phase one (2015–16): Main Stand redevelopment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anfield&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Phase one (2015–16): Main Stand redevelopment")]

In April 2014, Liverpool revealed plans for an expansion of the Main Stand, which involved adding a new third tier, new matchday facilities and enhanced corporate facilities. The new stand would add 8,500 seats and take the capacity of the stadium to 54,742.[[72]](#cite_note-vision-72)[[73]](#cite_note-73) Construction of the new Main Stand was made possible by the complete demolition of all the houses in Lothair Road as well as some of those on neighbouring Alroy Road and Anfield Road.[[74]](#cite_note-:0-74) Liverpool had started to purchase housing immediately surrounding Anfield in 2000.[[75]](#cite_note-:3-75) The houses were left empty, including at least 22 in the roads backing onto the main stand, and Liverpool were accused of deliberately allowing the area to become blighted, thus depressing the property prices.[[75]](#cite_note-:3-75)[[74]](#cite_note-:0-74)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Anfield_Main_Stand_exterior.jpg/250px-Anfield_Main_Stand_exterior.jpg)](/wiki/File:Anfield_Main_Stand_exterior.jpg)

Exterior of the completed Main Stand (pictured from [Stanley Park](/wiki/Stanley_Park,_Liverpool "Stanley Park, Liverpool"))

Work began on 8 December 2014, with the club aiming for the new stand to be match-ready and operational for the start of the [2016–17 season](/wiki/2016%E2%80%9317_Premier_League "2016–17 Premier League"). The work was undertaken by [Carillion](/wiki/Carillion "Carillion"). The structure of new stand was uniquely constructed around the existing main stand to enable the existing stand to continue to be used at full operational capacity during the 2015–16 season.[[76]](#cite_note-76) Demolition of the existing stand took place in summer 2016, allowing for the construction of the lower tiers of the new stand during the off-season. Consisting of 1.8 million bricks and blocks and over 5000 tonnes of steel, the stand was opened on schedule on 9 September 2016 for the first home game of the 2016–17 season, a 4–1 victory over [Leicester City](/wiki/Leicester_City_F.C. "Leicester City F.C."). Further internal construction work including new changing rooms and media facilities continued until April 2017.[[77]](#cite_note-77)[[78]](#cite_note-78)

#### New club superstore and matchday experience enhancements (2016–17)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anfield&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: New club superstore and matchday experience enhancements (2016–17)")]

In May 2016, outline planning permission was granted by Liverpool council for the construction of a new 1,800 sq m club superstore development, situated on Walton Breck Road on the corner of the Kop and the new Main Stand. Construction began in December 2016, with the store opening early in the 2017–18 season.[[79]](#cite_note-79) The space between the new store and the stadium was developed into a "fan zone", with new catering outlets and pre-match entertainment.[[80]](#cite_note-80)

#### Phase two (2021–2024): Anfield Road End redevelopment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anfield&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Phase two (2021–2024): Anfield Road End redevelopment")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/Anfield_Road_Stand_in_May_2024.jpg/250px-Anfield_Road_Stand_in_May_2024.jpg)](/wiki/File:Anfield_Road_Stand_in_May_2024.jpg)

The redeveloped Anfield Road End nearing completion in May 2024

The second phase of Anfield's redevelopment was to redevelop the Anfield Road stand. The club received initial outline planning permission in 2014 for the redevelopment, with seating to be increased by 4,825, giving Anfield a total capacity of 58,000.[[81]](#cite_note-81) However, in August 2019, Liverpool allowed the planning permission associated with the original 2014 designs to lapse, confirming their intention to submit "ambitious new plans" for the Anfield Road End redevelopment which are believed to increase the capacity further to take the overall capacity of Anfield to comfortably above 60,000.[[82]](#cite_note-82) The redevelopment focused on the upper tier of the stand, with the lower tier remaining unchanged. Construction work was completed behind the existing stand and then connected to the lower tier, which was expected to be in the 2022 off-season. Therefore, Anfield's capacity was not expected to be affected throughout the duration of the works.[[83]](#cite_note-83)

The club spent the second half of 2019 finalising plans and consulting local residents, planning officials and other stakeholders with a view to submitting the new plans for approval in early 2020.[[84]](#cite_note-84)[[85]](#cite_note-85) However, in March 2020, Liverpool delayed submitting a planning application for the project, in light of financial uncertainty caused by the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").[[86]](#cite_note-86)[[87]](#cite_note-:1-87) In December 2020, Liverpool announced that they would be moving forward with the project by submitting final planning application for the redevelopment, with the delay pushing back the initial completion date for Phase Two redevelopment from summer 2022 to summer 2023 at the earliest.[[87]](#cite_note-:1-87)[[88]](#cite_note-:2-88) The plans were approved by the [Liverpool City Council](/wiki/Liverpool_City_Council "Liverpool City Council") in June 2021. The expansion was expected to cost £60 million and allowed Anfield Road stand to seat 7,000 more people, giving the stadium a total capacity of 61,000.[[89]](#cite_note-89)

Work by contractor [Buckingham Group](/wiki/Buckingham_Group "Buckingham Group") officially commenced on 30 September 2021, with [the first sod](/wiki/Groundbreaking "Groundbreaking") being turned by manager [Jürgen Klopp](/wiki/J%C3%BCrgen_Klopp "Jürgen Klopp").[[90]](#cite_note-90) The stand was expected to be completed during the [2023–24 season](/wiki/2023%E2%80%9324_Premier_League "2023–24 Premier League") and would also see the relocation of the Family Park to a covered position.[[91]](#cite_note-expansion-91) However, on 17 August 2023, Buckingham announced it would be filing for administration, jeopardising timely completion of the new £80 million stand.[[92]](#cite_note-Hunter-17Aug2023-92)[[93]](#cite_note-BBC-17Aug2023-93) On 7 September 2023, the club took control of the project, appointing Rayner Rowen Construction to complete the stand using as many of the original subcontractors as possible.[[94]](#cite_note-Prior-08Sep2023-94) In October 2023, Liverpool announced that the upper part of the new developed stand would not be open to the public "until the end of 2023".[[95]](#cite_note-95) On 23 November, the club anticipated half of the new upper tier would be available for a Premier League match against Manchester United on 17 December 2023.[[96]](#cite_note-BBC-23Nov2023-96) Much of the stand opened for Liverpool's home Premier League game against [Burnley](/wiki/Burnley_F.C. "Burnley F.C.") on 10 February 2024.[[97]](#cite_note-97)

## Other uses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anfield&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Other uses")]

### International football and Liverpool Women

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anfield&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: International football and Liverpool Women")]

Anfield has hosted numerous international matches, and was one of the venues used during [UEFA Euro 1996](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1996 "UEFA Euro 1996"); the ground hosted three group games and a quarter-final.[[98]](#cite_note-98) The first international match hosted at Anfield was between [England](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team") and [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland_national_football_team_(1882%E2%80%931950) "Ireland national football team (1882–1950)"), in 1889. England won the match 6–1. Anfield was also the home venue for several of England's international football matches in the early 1900s, and for the [Welsh national team](/wiki/Wales_national_football_team "Wales national football team") in the later part of that century.[[99]](#cite_note-Kelly_118-99)[[100]](#cite_note-100) Anfield has also played host to five FA Cup semi-finals, the last of which was in 1929.[[99]](#cite_note-Kelly_118-99) The most recent international to be hosted at Anfield was England's 2–1 victory over [Uruguay](/wiki/Uruguay_national_football_team "Uruguay national football team") on 1 March 2006.[[101]](#cite_note-101) The close proximity of the stands to the pitch prevents the club from extending the pitch to suit UEFA's parameter requirements; since 2006, the stadium has been ineligible to host England games, Champions League and Europa League finals, and UEFA tournaments ([UEFA Euro 2028](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2028 "UEFA Euro 2028")).[[102]](#cite_note-102)

England has played two testimonial matches against Liverpool at Anfield. The first was in 1983, when England faced Liverpool for [Phil Thompson](/wiki/Phil_Thompson "Phil Thompson")'s testimonial. Then, in 1988, England visited again for [Alan Hansen](/wiki/Alan_Hansen "Alan Hansen")'s testimonial.[[103]](#cite_note-103) Liverpool's [arch rival](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C.%E2%80%93Manchester_United_F.C._rivalry "Liverpool F.C.–Manchester United F.C. rivalry") [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") played their first home game of the [1971–72](/wiki/1971%E2%80%9372_Manchester_United_F.C._season "1971–72 Manchester United F.C. season") season at Anfield as they were banned from playing their first two home league matches at [Old Trafford](/wiki/Old_Trafford "Old Trafford") after an incident of hooliganism. United beat [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.") 3–1.[[104]](#cite_note-104)

In November 2019, Anfield hosted a [Women's Super League](/wiki/FA_Women%27s_Super_League "FA Women's Super League") fixture for the first time, with the 6th matchday of the [2019–20 season](/wiki/2019%E2%80%9320_FA_WSL "2019–20 FA WSL") featuring the Merseyside Derby between [Liverpool Women](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C._Women "Liverpool F.C. Women") and local rivals [Everton Women](/wiki/Everton_F.C._(Women) "Everton F.C. (Women)").[[105]](#cite_note-105) On 25 September 2022, Anfield played host to second Merseyside Derby between two women's first teams in the Women's Super League. Played before a record attendance of 27,574 for a Liverpool game for their women's first team, it was also the first women's Merseyside Derby to be televised. This attendance would go on to become the 7th highest attendance of the [2022–23](/wiki/2022%E2%80%9323_Women%27s_Super_League "2022–23 Women's Super League") season.[[106]](#cite_note-106) On 22 August 2023, it was announced that Anfield would host a third women's Merseyside Derby on 15 October 2023.[[107]](#cite_note-107) On 14 March 2025, the women's team won at Anfield for the first time ever, beating Manchester United 3–1.[[108]](#cite_note-108)

### Rugby league

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anfield&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Rugby league")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/London_Broncos_2019_Magic_Weekend.jpg/250px-London_Broncos_2019_Magic_Weekend.jpg)](/wiki/File:London_Broncos_2019_Magic_Weekend.jpg)

[London Broncos](/wiki/London_Broncos "London Broncos") kicking off at Anfield during [Magic Weekend](/wiki/Magic_Weekend "Magic Weekend") 2019

The stadium has hosted five [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") matches: the 1989 [Charity Shield](/wiki/Rugby_League_Charity_Shield_(Great_Britain) "Rugby League Charity Shield (Great Britain)") between [Widnes](/wiki/Widnes_Vikings "Widnes Vikings") and [Wigan](/wiki/Wigan_Warriors "Wigan Warriors"),[[109]](#cite_note-109) the [1991 World Club Challenge](/wiki/1991_World_Club_Challenge "1991 World Club Challenge") between Wigan, winners of the [RFL Championship](/wiki/Rugby_Football_League_Championship "Rugby Football League Championship"), and [Penrith Panthers](/wiki/Penrith_Panthers "Penrith Panthers"), winners of the Australian [NSWRL Premiership](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Rugby_League_premiership "New South Wales Rugby League premiership"), with an attendance of 20,152;[[110]](#cite_note-four-nations-110) a 1997 [St. Helens](/wiki/St_Helens_R.F.C. "St Helens R.F.C.") [Super League](/wiki/Super_League_II "Super League II") home game against [Castleford Tigers](/wiki/Castleford_Tigers "Castleford Tigers"), with an attendance of 12,329;[[110]](#cite_note-four-nations-110) and the [2016 Rugby League Four Nations Final](/wiki/2016_Rugby_League_Four_Nations_Final "2016 Rugby League Four Nations Final"), in front of 40,042 people.[[110]](#cite_note-four-nations-110)

Anfield was chosen as the venue for the [2019 Magic Weekend](/wiki/Magic_Weekend#2019:_Liverpool "Magic Weekend") after two test matches were played there in 2016 and 2018. After previously choosing games that were local derbies or competitive games, in 2019 the fixtures were determined by the previous seasons league position. Robert Elstone, Super League Chief Executive, said "On behalf of the Super League clubs, we’re delighted to be taking the Dacia Magic Weekend to one of the most famous stadiums in the world.[[111]](#cite_note-111)

### Other sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anfield&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Other sports")]

Anfield has been the venue for many other events. During the mid-twenties, Anfield was the finishing line for the city [marathon](/wiki/Marathon "Marathon"). Liverpool held an annual race which started from St George's plateau in the city centre and finished with a lap of Anfield.[[99]](#cite_note-Kelly_118-99) [Boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing") matches were regularly held at Anfield during the inter-war years, including a number of British boxing championships; on 12 June 1934 [Nel Tarleton](/wiki/Nel_Tarleton "Nel Tarleton") beat [Freddie Miller](/wiki/Freddie_Miller_(boxer) "Freddie Miller (boxer)") for the [World Featherweight title](/wiki/List_of_featherweight_boxing_champions "List of featherweight boxing champions"). Professional tennis was played at Anfield on boards on the pitch. [US Open](/wiki/US_Open_(tennis) "US Open (tennis)") champion, [Bill Tilden](/wiki/Bill_Tilden "Bill Tilden"), and [Wimbledon](/wiki/The_Championships,_Wimbledon "The Championships, Wimbledon") champion, [Fred Perry](/wiki/Fred_Perry "Fred Perry"), entertained the crowds in an exhibition match. In 1958, an exhibition basketball match featuring the [Harlem Globetrotters](/wiki/Harlem_Globetrotters "Harlem Globetrotters") was held at the ground.[[112]](#cite_note-112)

### Non sporting events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anfield&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Non sporting events")]

Aside from sporting uses, Anfield has been a venue for musicians of different genres as well as evangelical preachers. One week in July 1984, the American [evangelist](/wiki/Evangelism "Evangelism") [Billy Graham](/wiki/Billy_Graham "Billy Graham") preached at Anfield, attracting crowds of over 30,000 each night.[[99]](#cite_note-Kelly_118-99) Anfield was featured in Liverpool's 2008 [European Capital of Culture](/wiki/European_Capital_of_Culture "European Capital of Culture") celebrations: 36,000 people attended a concert on 1 June 2008, featuring [the Zutons](/wiki/The_Zutons "The Zutons"), [Kaiser Chiefs](/wiki/Kaiser_Chiefs "Kaiser Chiefs") and [Paul McCartney](/wiki/Paul_McCartney "Paul McCartney").[[113]](#cite_note-113) Live concerts made a return to Anfield in the summer of 2019, with [Take That](/wiki/Take_That "Take That"), [Bon Jovi](/wiki/Bon_Jovi "Bon Jovi") and [Pink](/wiki/Pink_(singer) "Pink (singer)") performing. Take That lead singer [Gary Barlow](/wiki/Gary_Barlow "Gary Barlow"), a Liverpool fan, brought out a guest vocalist, [Gerry Marsden](/wiki/Gerry_Marsden "Gerry Marsden"), and they sang the club's anthem "[You'll Never Walk Alone](/wiki/You%27ll_Never_Walk_Alone "You'll Never Walk Alone")".[[114]](#cite_note-114) Artists who played at the stadium in summer 2022 include [Elton John](/wiki/Elton_John "Elton John"), [the Rolling Stones](/wiki/The_Rolling_Stones "The Rolling Stones") and the [Eagles](/wiki/Eagles_(band) "Eagles (band)").[[115]](#cite_note-115)[[116]](#cite_note-116)[[117]](#cite_note-117) [Taylor Swift](/wiki/Taylor_Swift "Taylor Swift") played three consecutive dates at Anfield, featuring [Paramore](/wiki/Paramore "Paramore") as special guests, in June 2024 as part of her [Eras Tour](/wiki/Eras_Tour "Eras Tour").[[118]](#cite_note-118) Artists who performed at the stadium in 2025 included [Dua Lipa](/wiki/Dua_Lipa "Dua Lipa"), [Lana Del Rey](/wiki/Lana_Del_Rey "Lana Del Rey") and [Bruce Springsteen](/wiki/Bruce_Springsteen "Bruce Springsteen").[[119]](#cite_note-119)

## Records

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anfield&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Records")]

[![graph showing troughs and peaks of attendance at Anfield](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Anfield_attendance_from_1946_to_2007.png/250px-Anfield_attendance_from_1946_to_2007.png)](/wiki/File:Anfield_attendance_from_1946_to_2007.png)

The average attendance at Anfield from 1946 to 2007

The highest sporting attendance recorded at Anfield is 61,905, for Liverpool's match against [Wolverhampton Wanderers](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Wanderers_F.C. "Wolverhampton Wanderers F.C.") in the FA Cup fifth round, on 2 February 1952.[[120]](#cite_note-Anfield-120) Since the Anfield Road end was expanded, Liverpool have seen bigger league attendances, and their most recent was 60,466 against Wolves on 27 December 2025.[[121]](#cite_note-121) The highest non-sporting attendance at Anfield was recorded during a Taylor Swift concert in June 2024 with over 62,000 in attendance.[[122]](#cite_note-122) The lowest attendance recorded at Anfield was 1,000 for a match against [Loughborough](/wiki/Loughborough_F.C. "Loughborough F.C.") on 7 December 1895.[[123]](#cite_note-123) The highest average attendance of 53,112 was set for the [2016–17](/wiki/2016%E2%80%9317_Liverpool_F.C._season "2016–17 Liverpool F.C. season") season.[[53]](#cite_note-timeline-53)

Liverpool did not lose a league match at Anfield during the [1893–94](/wiki/1893%E2%80%9394_in_English_football "1893–94 in English football"), [1970–71](/wiki/1970%E2%80%9371_in_English_football "1970–71 in English football"), [1976–77](/wiki/1976%E2%80%9377_in_English_football "1976–77 in English football"), [1978–79](/wiki/1978%E2%80%9379_in_English_football "1978–79 in English football"), [1979–80](/wiki/1979%E2%80%9380_in_English_football "1979–80 in English football"), [1987–88](/wiki/1987%E2%80%9388_in_English_football "1987–88 in English football"), [2008–09](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_in_English_football "2008–09 in English football"), [2017–18](/wiki/2017%E2%80%9318_in_English_football "2017–18 in English football"), [2018–19](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_in_English_football "2018–19 in English football"), [2019–20](/wiki/2019%E2%80%9320_in_English_football "2019–20 in English football") and [2021–22](/wiki/2021%E2%80%9322_in_English_football "2021–22 in English football") seasons. Liverpool's longest unbeaten streak at home extended from January 1978 to January 1981, a period encompassing 85 games, in which Liverpool scored 212 goals and conceded 35.[[120]](#cite_note-Anfield-120) The club's longest unbeaten home run in the league is 68 games, which occurred from April 2017 to January 2021.[[124]](#cite_note-124) Liverpool's worst losing streak at Anfield is six games in [2020–2021](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_Liverpool_F.C._season "2020–21 Liverpool F.C. season") with games played [behind closed doors](/wiki/Behind_closed_doors_(sport) "Behind closed doors (sport)") during the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").[[125]](#cite_note-125) The most consecutive league wins at Anfield is 24, this is the [longest run in English top-flight history](/wiki/Football_records_and_statistics_in_England#Wins "Football records and statistics in England"). It was accomplished across the 2018–19 and [2019–20 seasons](/wiki/2019%E2%80%9320_Liverpool_F.C._season "2019–20 Liverpool F.C. season").[[126]](#cite_note-126)

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anfield&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Transport")]

The stadium is about 2 miles (3 km) from [Lime Street Station](/wiki/Liverpool_Lime_Street_railway_station "Liverpool Lime Street railway station"),[[127]](#cite_note-127) which lies on a branch of the [West Coast Main Line](/wiki/West_Coast_Main_Line "West Coast Main Line") from [London Euston](/wiki/Euston_railway_station "Euston railway station"). [Kirkdale Station](/wiki/Kirkdale_railway_station "Kirkdale railway station"), about 1 mile (1.6 km) from the stadium, is the nearest station to Anfield. Fans travelling by train for matches may book direct to Anfield or Goodison Park, changing to the Peoplesbus Soccerbus service at [Sandhills Station](/wiki/Sandhills_railway_station "Sandhills railway station") on the [Merseyrail](/wiki/Merseyrail "Merseyrail") [Northern Line](/wiki/Northern_Line_(Merseyrail) "Northern Line (Merseyrail)").[[128]](#cite_note-128) The stadium has no parking facilities for supporters, and the streets around the ground allow parking only for residents with permits, although there are a small number of passes that can be allocated to over-65s. There are proposals under consideration for reinstating passenger traffic on the [Bootle Branch](/wiki/Canada_Dock_Branch "Canada Dock Branch"), which would cut the distance from the nearest railway station to about 0.5 miles (1 km).[[129]](#cite_note-129)
